narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses whether applicants are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, estimated an 78% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with strong odds of bar success to maintain high pass rates. The model's decision for you, as a Black male applicant, hinged most on your Year 3 school grades, which placed you in the bottom decile compared to peers—far below the typical mid-range performance that pushes predictions toward passing. This was the strongest factor pulling the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a significant drop-off in academic momentum by your later undergraduate years. Your LSAT score of 26 was the next biggest influence in the same direction, well under the average of around 37 seen in students who typically pass the bar, reinforcing concerns about your readiness for rigorous legal exams. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.10, though below the average 3.21, actually worked slightly in your favor by nudging the prediction a bit toward passing, likely because it showed some baseline consistency despite other challenges. Other details, like your middle family income bracket and part-time enrollment plans—which are less common than full-time among successful bar takers—played smaller roles but didn't outweigh the top concerns. Compared to typical admitted students, who often rank in the middle or higher deciles for grades and score much higher on the LSAT, your profile stood out as higher risk in these key areas. We understand this outcome can feel disheartening, especially given the personal hurdles many Black students like yourself face in competitive academic paths. To move forward, focus on retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the average, while strengthening your later-year grades through targeted coursework or tutoring if possible. Consider part-time programs or prep courses proven to help similar students improve bar outcomes. With these steps, you could reapply stronger next cycle and shift your predicted odds dramatically in your favor.
